Measuring the Persuasiveness
of Language Models
This memo is a summary of research conducted at Anthropic. Durmus, E., et al. (2024).
anthropic.com/news/measuring-model-persuasiveness
Policy highlights

new evaluation method to
measure the persuasiveness
of language modelsN
h Using this evaluation, we
found that our language
model, Claude 3 Opus, can
generate arguments that are
judged to be roughly as
persuasive as those written

making it easier for others to
build upon and use.
As the capabilities of AI models rapidly advance, there is growing interest
in understanding their potential to influence people's opinions and
decision-making. Measuring the persuasive capabilities of AI models is
important because persuasion may ultimately be tied to certain kinds of
misuse—such as using AI models to generate misinformation, or
persuading people to take actions against their own interests.

To date however, there has been limited empirical research into how
scaling up the size of models affects the persuasiveness of their outputs,
and how those compare to human-written content. In an effort to address
this, Anthropic developed a method to measure persuasiveness, and used
it to compare a variety of Anthropic language models. We found that
Claude 3 Opus produces arguments that don't statistically differ in their
persuasiveness compared to arguments written by humans. We also
found a clear scaling trend across model generations: each successive
model generation is more persuasive than the previous.
   



 

Experimental design: Evaluating persuasion
on emerging, less-polarized topics

To evaluate persuasiveness, we created an experimental setup where
people interact with a language model (chatbot) in a one-turn
conversation. A person is given an opinionated claim on an emerging

Claude 1.3 	Claude 2 	Claude 3

Model Persuasiveness (higher is more persuasive)

-- 1 of 2 --

policy topic (e.g., “Emotional AI companions should be
regulated”) and asked to report their initial level of support
on a 1-7 scale (from “completely opposed” to “completely
support”). They are then presented with a short (~250
word) argument in support of that claim, written by either
a human or a language model, and asked to rate their
stance on the original claim once again.
We measure the shift in people’s stances between their
initial view on a particular claim and their view after
reading a supporting argument for that claim, and use that
to compute a persuasiveness metric (i.e., the difference
between final and initial support score). Larger increases
in final support scores indicate that a given argument is
more effective in shifting people’s viewpoints, while
smaller changes suggest less persuasive arguments.

Results: AI models are becoming
increasingly persuasive

Through our experiment, we found that our language
model Claude 3 Opus was judged to be roughly as
persuasive as humans in changing people’s opinions on
nuanced topics. While human-written arguments were
rated as slightly more persuasive than AI-generated
arguments, Claude 3 Opus achieved a comparable
persuasiveness score with no statistically significant
difference between the two. Additionally (and perhaps
more informative for anticipating future AI capabilities),
we observed a general scaling trend across model
generations, with newer models becoming increasingly
persuasive.
Challenges in studying the
persuasiveness of AI models

Assessing the impacts of persuasive AI models is
inherently difficult. Persuasion is a nuanced phenomenon
shaped by many subjective factors, and is further
complicated by ethical considerations and the bounds of
experimental design. People's viewpoints are shaped by
their total lived experiences, social circles, and trusted
information sources—reading isolated written arguments
in an experiment setting may not fully capture the ways in
which people change their minds.
More importantly, the ability to quantify societal concerns
around potential misuse in real-world settings is ethically
fraught. Studying the risks of persuasion “in the wild,”
such as the effects of AI-generated disinformation, would
present an unacceptable and unethical risk of concrete
harm. Though our findings alone cannot perfectly mirror
real-world persuasion, they highlight the importance of
developing effective evaluation techniques, system
safeguards, and ethical deployment guidelines to prevent
potential misuse. In addition to our research on the topic,
we explicitly prohibit the use of our models for activities
where persuasive content could be harmful, and
complement those policies with monitoring and
enforcement systems.

Sharing tools to measure the
persuasiveness of AI models

Measuring the persuasiveness of AI models—and how that
changes as a function of model scale—is a critical yet
underexplored research area. Understanding how AI
models might influence people’s views can help us
anticipate the societal impacts of these systems and
ensure their responsible development and use. Towards
this end, we developed a novel evaluation technique, and
publicly released the associated data so that others may
use and build upon it. We hope that in sharing these
findings, we can foster further research and policy
discussions that work towards developing effective
guidelines, safeguards, and best practices for the
responsible deployment of increasingly
persuasive AI models.
About us
Anthropic is a public benefit corporation and
AI safety research company that is working
to build reliable, interpretable, and
steerable AI systems.
April 2024
